passage: Ecologists studying the coral reefs of the Indo-Pacific have documented a troubling pattern, bleaching events that once occurred once every twenty-five to thirty years now occur every five to six years on average, leaving the reefs insufficient time to recover between episodes.
question: Which choice correctly fixes the sentence boundary error in the passage?

choices.A: Ecologists studying the coral reefs of the Indo-Pacific have documented a troubling pattern: bleaching events that once occurred every twenty-five to thirty years now occur every five to six years on average, leaving the reefs insufficient time to recover between episodes.
choices.B: Ecologists studying the coral reefs of the Indo-Pacific have documented a troubling pattern bleaching events that once occurred every twenty-five to thirty years now occur every five to six years, leaving the reefs insufficient time to recover.
choices.C: Ecologists studying the coral reefs of the Indo-Pacific have documented a troubling pattern; bleaching events that once occurred every twenty-five to thirty years now occur every five to six years on average.
choices.D: No change; the comma after 'troubling pattern' is correct because it introduces a relative clause that defines the pattern.

explanation: The passage contains a comma splice—two independent clauses joined by only a comma. 'Ecologists...have documented a troubling pattern' is a complete independent clause, and 'bleaching events that once occurred once every twenty-five to thirty years now occur every five to six years on average' is a second complete independent clause. The first clause ends with 'pattern,' which is a noun that the second clause directly defines. A colon is the best punctuation here because it introduces an explanation or definition of the 'troubling pattern' identified in the first clause. Choice A uses a colon. Choice B creates a fused sentence. Choice C uses a semicolon and omits the participial phrase. Choice D incorrectly defends the comma splice.
